Description: Each view should have a single responsibility for better readability and reusability
Do: Extract complex views into smaller subviews with clear purposes
Don't: Create views with hundreds of lines of code
Code Good: struct TraderCard: View { let trader: Trader; var body: some View { VStack { AvatarView(url: trader.avatar); StatsView(stats: trader.stats) } } }
Code Bad: struct ContentView: View { var body: some View { /* 500 lines */ } }

Description: ViewBuilder allows returning different view types based on conditions
Do: Use @ViewBuilder for methods that return conditional views
Don't: Force type erasure with AnyView for conditionals
Code Good: @ViewBuilder func content() -> some View { if isLoggedIn { HomeView() } else { LoginView() } }
Code Bad: func content() -> AnyView { if isLoggedIn { return AnyView(HomeView()) } else { return AnyView(LoginView()) } }

Description: Compose views by combining smaller views rather than creating deep hierarchies
Do: Build complex UIs by composing simple reusable views
Don't: Create complex view inheritance chains
Code Good: struct PrimaryButton: View { var body: some View { Button(action: action) { Text(title) }.buttonStyle(.borderedProminent) } }
Code Bad: // Trying to subclass Button

Guideline: Use custom view modifiers for reusable styling
Description: Custom ViewModifiers encapsulate common styling patterns for reuse
Do: Create custom modifiers for styles used in multiple places
Don't: Duplicate styling code across views
Code Good: struct CardStyle: ViewModifier { func body(content: Content) -> some View { content.padding().background(Color.white).cornerRadius(12).shadow(radius: 4) } } view.modifier(CardStyle())
Code Bad: VStack { ... }.padding().background(Color.white).cornerRadius(12).shadow(radius: 4) // Repeated everywhere

Description: Use onAppear onDisappear and task for view lifecycle management
Do: Perform side effects in lifecycle methods not in body
Don't: Put side effects in the body property
Code Good: struct TraderView: View { var body: some View { List(traders) { ... }.onAppear { loadTraders() }.task { await fetchData() } } }
Code Bad: var body: some View { let _ = loadData(); List { ... } }

Description: @State is for simple value types owned by a single view
Do: Use @State for private view-internal state like toggles or text input
Don't: Use @State for shared state or complex objects
Code Good: struct ToggleView: View { @State private var isOn = false; var body: some View { Toggle(isOn: $isOn) { Text('Setting') } } }
Code Bad: struct ToggleView: View { @State var sharedData = ComplexObject() }

Description: @Binding creates a reference to state owned by a parent view
Do: Pass bindings to child views that need to modify parent state
Don't: Pass values and callbacks instead of bindings when binding is cleaner
Code Good: struct ChildView: View { @Binding var text: String; var body: some View { TextField('Enter', text: $text) } }
Code Bad: struct ChildView: View { var text: String; var onTextChange: (String) -> Void }

Description: @State should be private as it represents view-internal state
Do: Always use private with @State to indicate view ownership
Don't: Make @State properties internal or public
Code Good: @State private var count = 0
Code Bad: @State var count = 0 // Can be set externally

Description: Leaf views should receive data via parameters not manage their own state
Do: Keep state at the appropriate level and pass values to children
Don't: Give every view its own @State that could be derived
Code Good: struct TraderRow: View { let trader: Trader; var body: some View { Text(trader.name) } }
Code Bad: struct TraderRow: View { @State var traderName: String; init(trader: Trader) { _traderName = State(initialValue: trader.name) } }

Description: @StateObject creates and owns an ObservableObject ensuring single initialization
Do: Use @StateObject when a view creates and owns an ObservableObject
Don't: Use @ObservedObject for objects the view creates
Code Good: struct ParentView: View { @StateObject private var viewModel = TraderViewModel(); var body: some View { ChildView(viewModel: viewModel) } }
Code Bad: struct ParentView: View { @ObservedObject var viewModel = TraderViewModel() // Recreated on each render }

Description: @Observable macro provides simpler observation than ObservableObject
Do: Use @Observable with @Bindable for iOS 17+ apps
Don't: Use ObservableObject for new iOS 17+ projects
Code Good: @Observable class TraderStore { var traders: [Trader] = []; func fetch() async { ... } } struct View { @Bindable var store: TraderStore }
Code Bad: class TraderStore: ObservableObject { @Published var traders: [Trader] = [] } // For iOS 17+

Description: @ObservedObject is for ObservableObjects passed from a parent view
Do: Use @ObservedObject when receiving an existing ObservableObject from parent
Don't: Create ObservableObject inside @ObservedObject declaration
Code Good: struct ChildView: View { @ObservedObject var store: TraderStore; var body: some View { List(store.traders) { ... } } }
Code Bad: struct ChildView: View { @ObservedObject var store = TraderStore() }

Description: @EnvironmentObject passes ObservableObjects through the environment
Do: Use environment objects for data needed by many views at different levels
Don't: Pass objects through many view layers manually
Code Good: struct RootView: View { @StateObject var auth = AuthStore(); var body: some View { ContentView().environmentObject(auth) } } struct DeepView: View { @EnvironmentObject var auth: AuthStore }
Code Bad: struct A: View { var auth: AuthStore; var body: some View { B(auth: auth) } } struct B: View { var auth: AuthStore; var body: some View { C(auth: auth) } }

Description: Only mark properties that need observation with @Published
Do: Use @Published only for properties that should trigger view updates
Don't: Mark all properties as @Published
Code Good: class ViewModel: ObservableObject { @Published var traders: [Trader] = []; private var cache: [String: Data] = [:] // Not published }
Code Bad: class ViewModel: ObservableObject { @Published var traders: [Trader] = []; @Published var cache: [String: Data] = [:] // Causes unnecessary updates }

Description: Choose the right stack container for your layout direction needs
Do: Use HStack for horizontal VStack for vertical ZStack for overlapping
Don't: Force layouts with offsets instead of using appropriate stacks
Code Good: HStack { Image(systemName: 'star'); Text('Rating'); Spacer() }
Code Bad: ZStack { Text('Rating').offset(x: 30); Image(systemName: 'star') }

Guideline: Use LazyVStack and LazyHStack for long lists
Description: Lazy stacks only render visible content providing better performance
Do: Use Lazy variants inside ScrollView for lists with many items
Don't: Use VStack inside ScrollView for long lists
Code Good: ScrollView { LazyVStack { ForEach(traders) { trader in TraderRow(trader: trader) } } }
Code Bad: ScrollView { VStack { ForEach(traders) { trader in TraderRow(trader: trader) } } }

Description: GeometryReader should be used only when you need parent dimensions
Do: Use GeometryReader for truly responsive layouts that need measurements
Don't: Use GeometryReader for simple layouts that work with native sizing
Code Good: GeometryReader { geo in Image('background').resizable().frame(width: geo.size.width, height: geo.size.height * 0.3) }
Code Bad: GeometryReader { geo in Text('Hello').frame(width: 100) // Didn't need GeometryReader }

Description: Set explicit alignment and spacing for consistent layouts
Do: Specify alignment and spacing parameters for stacks
Don't: Rely on default alignment for complex layouts
Code Good: VStack(alignment: .leading, spacing: 8) { Text('Title'); Text('Subtitle') }
Code Bad: VStack { Text('Title'); Text('Subtitle') } // Default centered alignment

Description: Modifier order matters as each modifier wraps the previous result
Do: Apply modifiers in logical order understanding their cumulative effect
Don't: Ignore modifier order assuming they are independent
Code Good: Text('Hello').padding().background(Color.blue) // Padding inside blue background
Code Bad: Text('Hello').background(Color.blue).padding() // Blue only behind text

Description: Frame sets the proposed size for a view and its alignment
Do: Use frame with alignment parameter for positioning
Don't: Use frame when intrinsic sizing would work
Code Good: Text('Centered').frame(maxWidth: .infinity, alignment: .center)
Code Bad: Text('Hello').frame(width: 200, height: 50) // When content fits naturally

Description: Extension methods make view configuration more readable and reusable
Do: Create view extensions for common modifier combinations
Don't: Repeat the same modifier chains throughout codebase
Code Good: extension View { func cardStyle() -> some View { self.padding().background(Color.white).cornerRadius(12).shadow(radius: 4) } } someView.cardStyle()
Code Bad: someView.padding().background(Color.white).cornerRadius(12).shadow(radius: 4) // Repeated

Description: safeAreaInset adds custom UI that respects safe areas like a floating button
Do: Use safeAreaInset for persistent UI elements that shouldn't overlap content
Don't: Use ZStack overlay for elements that should affect content layout
Code Good: NavigationStack { List { ... }.safeAreaInset(edge: .bottom) { ActionBar() } }
Code Bad: ZStack(alignment: .bottom) { List { ... }; ActionBar() } // List content hidden behind bar

Description: NavigationStack provides programmatic navigation with type-safe paths
Do: Use NavigationStack with navigationDestination for modern navigation
Don't: Use deprecated NavigationView for new iOS 16+ projects
Code Good: NavigationStack { List(traders) { trader in NavigationLink(trader.name, value: trader) }.navigationDestination(for: Trader.self) { trader in TraderDetailView(trader: trader) } }
Code Bad: NavigationView { NavigationLink(destination: DetailView()) { Text('Go') } }

Guideline: Use navigation path for programmatic navigation
Description: NavigationPath enables programmatic navigation control
Do: Use NavigationPath for complex navigation flows with programmatic control
Don't: Hard-code navigation structure without programmatic option
Code Good: @State private var path = NavigationPath(); NavigationStack(path: $path) { ... } func goToTrader(_ id: String) { path.append(TraderId(id)) }
Code Bad: // No programmatic navigation possible

Description: Present modal content with the appropriate presentation style
Do: Use sheet for partial coverage and fullScreenCover for immersive experiences
Don't: Always use fullScreenCover when sheet would be more appropriate
Code Good: Button('Details') { showDetails = true }.sheet(isPresented: $showDetails) { DetailSheet() }
Code Bad: Button('Details') { showDetails = true }.fullScreenCover(isPresented: $showDetails) { DetailSheet() } // For simple form

Description: Configure your app to handle incoming URLs and navigate appropriately
Do: Implement onOpenURL to handle deep links and update navigation state
Don't: Ignore deep linking requirements
Code Good: NavigationStack(path: $path) { ... }.onOpenURL { url in if let traderId = parseTraderDeepLink(url) { path.append(TraderId(traderId)) } }
Code Bad: // No deep link handling

Guideline: Use List for standard list UI
Description: List provides native platform list styling and behavior automatically
Do: Use List for standard scrolling lists with platform-appropriate appearance
Don't: Use ScrollView with LazyVStack when List styling is needed
Code Good: List(traders) { trader in TraderRow(trader: trader) }
Code Bad: ScrollView { LazyVStack { ForEach(traders) { trader in TraderRow(trader: trader).listRowStyle() } } }

Description: ForEach requires identifiable data for efficient diffing
Do: Make model types conform to Identifiable or provide id parameter
Don't: Use indices for ForEach identification
Code Good: struct Trader: Identifiable { let id: String; ... } ForEach(traders) { trader in ... }
Code Bad: ForEach(0..<traders.count) { i in TraderRow(trader: traders[i]) }

Description: Swipe actions provide quick access to common row operations
Do: Add swipe actions for common operations like delete or favorite
Don't: Build custom swipe gesture handling
Code Good: List { ForEach(traders) { trader in TraderRow(trader: trader).swipeActions { Button(role: .destructive) { delete(trader) } label: { Label('Delete', systemImage: 'trash') } } } }
Code Bad: // Custom pan gesture for swipe actions

Description: The searchable modifier adds standard search UI to lists
Do: Use searchable modifier for filterable lists
Don't: Build custom search bar UI
Code Good: NavigationStack { List(filteredTraders) { ... }.searchable(text: $searchText, prompt: 'Search traders') }
Code Bad: VStack { TextField('Search', text: $searchText); List(filteredTraders) { ... } }

Guideline: Use Form for settings and input screens
Description: Form provides platform-appropriate styling for settings and data entry
Do: Use Form with Section for organized input screens
Don't: Build forms manually with VStack and custom styling
Code Good: Form { Section('Account') { TextField('Name', text: $name); Toggle('Notifications', isOn: $notifs) } }
Code Bad: VStack { Text('Account'); TextField('Name', text: $name); Toggle('Notifications', isOn: $notifs) }

Description: Choose the right control for each input type
Do: Match input control to data type like Picker Toggle Slider
Don't: Use TextField for all input types
Code Good: Picker('Exchange', selection: $exchange) { ForEach(exchanges, id: \.self) { Text($0) } }
Code Bad: TextField('Exchange', text: $exchange) // When there are fixed options

Description: Show validation errors clearly near the relevant fields
Do: Display inline validation errors with clear messaging
Don't: Submit forms without validation or show generic errors
Code Good: TextField('Email', text: $email); if !isValidEmail { Text('Please enter valid email').foregroundColor(.red).font(.caption) }
Code Bad: // No validation feedback until submit

Guideline: Use task modifier for async operations
Description: The task modifier manages async work tied to view lifecycle
Do: Use task modifier for async operations that should start with the view
Don't: Call Task {} in onAppear
Code Good: List(traders).task { await loadTraders() }
Code Bad: List(traders).onAppear { Task { await loadTraders() } }

Description: Use AsyncImage and custom phase handling for async content
Do: Show loading error and success states for async operations
Don't: Show nothing or crash during async loading
Code Good: AsyncImage(url: url) { phase in switch phase { case .success(let image): image; case .failure: Image(systemName: 'exclamationmark.triangle'); case .empty: ProgressView(); @unknown default: EmptyView() } }
Code Bad: AsyncImage(url: url) // No loading or error handling

Description: Tasks started with task modifier are cancelled automatically but manual tasks need handling
Do: Let task modifier handle cancellation or implement manual cancellation for custom tasks
Don't: Start async work that continues after view disappears
Code Good: // Task modifier handles cancellation automatically Text('').task { await longRunningWork() }
Code Bad: // Manual Task that's never cancelled onAppear { Task { await longRunningWork() } }

Description: withAnimation wraps state changes to animate resulting view updates
Do: Wrap state changes that should animate in withAnimation
Don't: Apply animation modifier to everything
Code Good: Button('Toggle') { withAnimation(.spring()) { isExpanded.toggle() } }
Code Bad: Button('Toggle') { isExpanded.toggle() }.animation(.spring(), value: isExpanded) // Animation on every change

Guideline: Use animation modifier with value parameter
Description: Specify which value changes should trigger animation to avoid unexpected animations
Do: Use animation(_:value:) to scope animations to specific state changes
Don't: Use deprecated animation modifier without value
Code Good: Text(count.description).animation(.default, value: count)
Code Bad: Text(count.description).animation(.default) // Deprecated and unpredictable

Description: matchedGeometryEffect creates smooth transitions between views with shared elements
Do: Use matchedGeometryEffect for hero animations between states
Don't: Build complex animations manually for shared element transitions
Code Good: @Namespace var animation; if isExpanded { ExpandedCard().matchedGeometryEffect(id: 'card', in: animation) } else { CollapsedCard().matchedGeometryEffect(id: 'card', in: animation) }
Code Bad: // Manual frame animations for expand/collapse

Guideline: Create multiple previews for different states
Description: Preview different configurations to catch edge cases during development
Do: Create previews for loading error empty and populated states
Don't: Only preview happy path state
Code Good: #Preview('Empty') { TraderList(traders: []) } #Preview('Loading') { TraderList(isLoading: true) } #Preview('Populated') { TraderList(traders: mockTraders) }
Code Bad: #Preview { TraderList(traders: mockTraders) } // Only one state

Description: Create mock data specifically for previews to show realistic UI
Do: Create static preview fixtures that showcase different scenarios
Don't: Use production data fetching in previews
Code Good: #Preview { TraderCard(trader: .preview) } extension Trader { static let preview = Trader(id: '1', name: 'Top Trader', roi: 150.5) }
Code Bad: #Preview { TraderCard(trader: await API.fetchTrader()) }

Description: Test your UI on different device sizes and configurations
Do: Create previews for different devices and size classes
Don't: #Preview('iPhone SE') { ContentView().previewDevice('iPhone SE') } #Preview('iPad') { ContentView().previewDevice('iPad Pro 12.9-inch') }
Code Good: #Preview { ContentView() } // Only one device
Code Bad: info

Description: Structure views to minimize recalculation when state changes
Do: Break views into smaller pieces so only affected parts recalculate
Don't: Put all state at the top level causing full view recalculation
Code Good: struct ParentView: View { var body: some View { ExpensiveView(); CounterView() // Only counter recalculates } } struct CounterView: View { @State var count = 0 }
Code Bad: struct ParentView: View { @State var count = 0; var body: some View { ExpensiveView(); Text('\\(count)') // ExpensiveView recalculates too } }

Description: Equatable conformance can help SwiftUI skip unnecessary view updates
Do: Conform views to Equatable when identity comparison is cheaper than body evaluation
Don't: Implement Equatable without measuring actual performance benefit
Code Good: struct TraderRow: View, Equatable { let trader: Trader; static func == (lhs: Self, rhs: Self) -> Bool { lhs.trader.id == rhs.trader.id } }
Code Bad: // Implement Equatable on all views without profiling

Description: Use Instruments to identify SwiftUI performance issues
Do: Profile your app to find actual bottlenecks before optimizing
Don't: Guess at performance issues without measurement
Code Good: // Use Instruments SwiftUI template to profile view updates
Code Bad: // Random optimization without profiling

Description: Provide meaningful labels for VoiceOver users
Do: Add accessibility labels to images buttons and custom controls
Don't: Button { submit() } label: { Image(systemName: 'paperplane') }.accessibilityLabel('Send message')
Code Good: Button { submit() } label: { Image(systemName: 'paperplane') } // No label
Code Bad: error

Description: Respect user font size preferences for better accessibility
Do: Use built-in text styles and test with larger accessibility sizes
Don't: Text('Title').font(.headline) // Scales with Dynamic Type
Code Good: Text('Title').font(.system(size: 18)) // Fixed size ignores Dynamic Type
Code Bad: warning

Description: Combine related elements into single accessibility elements when appropriate
Do: Use accessibilityElement and accessibilityChildren for better VoiceOver experience
Don't: HStack { Image('star'); Text('4.5 rating') }.accessibilityElement(children: .combine)
Code Good: // Each element read separately
Code Bad: info

Description: Test SwiftUI views with ViewInspector for unit testing UI
Do: Write unit tests for view behavior and structure
Don't: Only test views manually
Code Good: func testTraderRowShowsName() throws { let view = TraderRow(trader: .mock); let text = try view.inspect().find(text: 'MockTrader'); XCTAssertNotNil(text) }
Code Bad: // No automated view tests

Description: Separate view logic from presentation using a clear architecture pattern
Do: Keep views focused on UI and move logic to view models or services
Don't: Put all business logic directly in views
Code Good: @Observable class TraderViewModel { var traders: [Trader] = []; func fetch() async { traders = await service.getTraders() } } struct TraderList: View { @Bindable var vm: TraderViewModel }
Code Bad: struct TraderList: View { @State var traders: [Trader] = []; func fetch() async { /* API logic here */ } }
